tldr: LightGBM baseline for NCAA tournament prediction using season win ratios, score gaps, and seed differences, validated via time-based sequential splits and averaged across folds.
summary: This notebook implements a LightGBM baseline for predicting NCAA tournament game outcomes. It constructs team-level seasonal features (win ratio, average score gap) from regular season and tournament results, then merges seed information. The dataset is symmetrized to handle both winning and losing matchups, and pairwise differences are computed. The model is trained using a time-based sequential validation scheme, with predictions averaged across folds to generate the final submission.
extensive_summary:
## Data Preparation
The notebook loads historical NCAA tournament seeds and regular season/tournament compact results for both men's and women's divisions, concatenating them into unified DataFrames. It drops the `NumOT` and `WLoc` columns and computes a `ScoreGap` feature (`WScore - LScore`).

## Feature Engineering
For each team and season, the author computes aggregations: `NumWins`, `NumLosses`, `GapWins` (mean score gap in wins), and `GapLosses` (mean score gap in losses). From these, two derived features are created: `WinRatio` (wins divided by total games) and `GapAvg` (a weighted score gap difference). These are merged for both the winning (`WTeamID`) and losing (`LTeamID`) teams.

The training data is filtered to seasons `>= 2017`. Seeds are parsed by stripping non-numeric characters to convert them into integers. To handle matchup symmetry, the dataset is duplicated: winning matches are kept as-is, while losing matches are flipped so that the losing team becomes `TeamIdA` and the winning team becomes `TeamIdB`. Columns are suffixed with `A` and `B`. Pairwise differences are then calculated: `SeedDiff`, `WinRatioDiff`, and `GapAvgDiff`. The target variable is binary: `WinA = 1` if `ScoreDiff > 0`, else `0`.

## Modeling & Validation
The final feature set includes `SeedA`, `SeedB`, `WinRatioA`, `GapAvgA`, `WinRatioB`, `GapAvgB`, and their respective differences. Missing values are imputed with training set medians. Features are min-max scaled using training set bounds.

A LightGBM classifier is trained with `objective='binary'`, `num_leaves=31`, `learning_rate=0.05`, `feature_fraction=0.8`, `bagging_fraction=0.8`, `bagging_freq=5`, and `n_estimators=1000` with `early_stopping_rounds=50`. Validation follows a time-based sequential scheme: the model trains on all prior seasons and validates on the current season iteratively. Validation predictions are min-max scaled again and clipped to `[0, 1]`. The final submission averages the out-of-fold test predictions across all validation folds.
cv_strategy: Time-based sequential validation (train on all prior seasons, validate on current season iteratively)
preprocessing: Min-max scaling (fit on train, applied to val/test)
feature_engineering: Group-by aggregations (NumWins, NumLosses, mean score gaps per team/season)
ensemble: Averages out-of-fold predictions across time-based validation folds.
post_processing: Min-max scaling of validation predictions, clipping to [0, 1], averaging fold predictions for final submission.
notable_techniques: Symmetrical data duplication to treat wins/losses as A/B pairs
Time-based sequential CV for temporal data
Min-max scaling fitted on training folds only
Averaging out-of-fold predictions for final submission
